mainTopic: (continues) טופס ותורף · המגביה מציאה לחבירו · רכוב ומנהיג
overview: (1) The Gemara discusses the division of a document between the main body (טופס) and the remainder (תורף), and whether they are divided or taken separately. (2) The Gemara debates whether lifting a found object for a partner acquires it for them, analyzing the principle of 'מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה'. (3) The Gemara discusses the laws of acquisition when one rides an animal and another leads it, analyzing the status of the rider (רכוב) versus the leader (מנהיג) and the concept of 'מוסירה'. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses the division of a document between the main body (טופס) and the remainder (תורף), and whether they are divided or taken separately.
The Gemara debates whether lifting a found object for a partner acquires it for them, analyzing the principle of 'מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה'.

The Gemara discusses the laws of acquisition when one rides an animal and another leads it, analyzing the status of the rider (רכוב) versus the leader (מנהיג) and the concept of 'מוסירה'. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Challenge from רבה's ruling on a golden garment
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the rejection by citing רבה's ruling that a golden garment is divided, which would seem to ruin it.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new challenge using רבה's ruling on a golden garment. This is a transition line introducing a specific challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבה rules that a golden garment is divided, which seems to contradict the principle that physical division is not always feasible. If a golden garment is divided, it would be ruined, challenging the previous rejection.

phrases.english: And the one who said Rava:
If the garment was gold-threaded –

phrases.aramaic: חוֹלְקִין, הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי לַהּ, הָא אַפְסְדוּהָ!
phrases.english: They divide, even if they split it, behold they ruined it!

phrases.aramaic: הָא לָא קַשְׁיָא, דְּחַזְיָא לִבְנֵי מְלָכִים.
phrases.english: This is not difficult, that it is fit for kings' sons.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָא דְּאָמַר רָבָא: אִם הָיְתְּה טַלִּית מוּזְהֶבֶת – חוֹלְקִין, הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי לַהּ, הָא אַפְסְדוּהָ!

rashi.hebrew: דחזיא לבני מלכים - לבנים קטנים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase חזיא לבני מלכים means the garment is suitable for small children, implying it can be divided without ruining its value for that specific use.

steps.title: Challenge from the משנה on two riding an animal
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the rejection by citing the Mishnah on two people riding an animal, which would seem to ruin the animal if divided.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new challenge using the משנה on two people riding an animal. This is a transition line introducing a specific challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The משנה discusses two people riding an animal, and if the animal is divided, it would be ruined. This challenges the previous rejection that physical division is always feasible.

keyTerms.term: הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רֹכְבִים עַל גַּבֵּי בְהֵמָה

phrases.english: And what about that which we learned:

phrases.aramaic: הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה וְכוּ׳,
phrases.english: Two were riding on an animal, etc.,

phrases.aramaic: הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי לַהּ? הָא אַפְסְדוּהָ!
phrases.english: Here too, if they divide it? They have ruined it!
If it is ritually pure – it is fit for meat.
But if it is ritually impure, they have ruined it!
But for its value, here too for its value.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָא דִּתְנַן: הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה וְכוּ׳, הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי לַהּ? הָא אַפְסְדוּהָ! בִּשְׁלָמָא טְהוֹרָה – חַזְיָא לְבָשָׂר. אֶלָּא טְמֵאָה, הָא אַפְסְדוּהָ!

steps.title: רמי בר חמא's derivation from the משנה
steps.stepSummary: רמי בר חמא derives from the Mishnah that lifting a found object for a partner acquires it for them.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with רמי בר חמא stating a halakhic principle derived from the משנה, followed by his logical proof. This is a setup line introducing a new principle.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רמי בר חמא argues that if lifting for another didn't acquire, the משנה's case of two holding a garment would mean neither acquires. Since the משנה implies acquisition, lifting for another must be effective.

keyTerms.meaning: One who lifts a found object for his partner

steps.whatToRemember: The core principle: המגביה מציאה לחבירו – קנה חבירו.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline view that agency in acquisition (מגביה לחבירו) is valid for found objects.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא: זֹאת אוֹמֶרֶת,
phrases.english: Rami bar Hama says: This teaches,
One who lifts a found object for his fellow — his fellow acquires it.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי סָלְקָא דַּעְתָּךְ לֹא קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ,
phrases.english: For if your mind enters that his fellow does not acquire it,

phrases.aramaic: תֵּיעָשֶׂה זוֹ כְּמִי שֶׁמּוּנַּחַת עַל גַּבֵּי קַרְקַע,
phrases.english: this would be as one who is lying on the ground,
phrases.notes: Refers to the portion of the garment held by one litigant.

phrases.aramaic: וְזוֹ כְּמִי שֶׁמּוּנַּחַת עַל גַּבֵּי קַרְקַע,
phrases.english: and this as one who is lying on the ground,
phrases.notes: Refers to the portion of the garment held by the other litigant.

phrases.aramaic: וְלֹא יִקְנֶה לֹא זֶה וְלֹא זֶה.
phrases.english: and neither this one nor that one acquires it.
Rather, do we not hear from it:
One who lifts a found object for his fellow — his fellow acquires it.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָמֵי בַּר חָמָא: זֹאת אוֹמֶרֶת, הַמַּגְבִּיהַּ מְצִיאָה לַחֲבֵירוֹ – קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: זאת אומרת - מתני' דקתני שנים שהגביהו מציאה קנאוה ויחלוקו וכשמגביה מגביה לדעת שיקנה בה חבירו חציה ש"מ המגביה מציאה כו' ולקמיה פריך דיוקא דרמי בר חמא מהיכא מאיזו בבא ממשנתנו הוא למד כן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רמי בר חמא derives the principle from the Mishnah's case of two people lifting a מציאה together, where each lifts with the intent that the other acquires half, proving that lifting for another is effective.

rashi.hebrew: ולא יקנה לא זה ולא זה - וכל הרוצה יחטפנה מידם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that if neither person acquired the item by lifting, it would remain as if on the ground, meaning anyone could seize it from them.

steps.title: רבה's challenge to the derivation
steps.stepSummary: רבה challenges רמי בר חמא's derivation, arguing that lifting for another does not acquire, and the Mishnah's case works differently.
steps.whatsHappening: רבה objects to the previous derivation, offering an alternative explanation for the משנה. This is a transition line introducing a challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבה argues that the משנה's case works because each person acquires for themselves, and by extension, they acquire for each other. This undermines the need for the principle of lifting for another.

keyTerms.term: מִגּוֹ דְזָכִי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכִי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ
keyTerms.meaning: Since he acquires for himself, he also acquires for his partner
His partner does not acquire it

steps.whatToRemember: רבה's principle: מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shifts the debate from agency in acquisition to the scope of self-acquisition extending to others.

phrases.english: Rava said: Let me say to you forever:
One who lifts a found object for his friend –
his friend does not acquire.
And here this is the reason:

phrases.aramaic: מִגּוֹ דְּזָכֵי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכֵי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: From the fact that he acquires for himself, he also acquires for his friend.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא, לְעוֹלָם אֵימָא לָךְ: הַמַּגְבִּיהַּ מְצִיאָה לַחֲבֵירוֹ – לֹא קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ.

rashi.hebrew: לעולם אימא לך לא קנה חבירו - היכא דמגביה לא נתכוין לקנות בה כלום וטעמא אמר לקמן (בבא מציעא דף י.) דהוי תופס לבעל חוב במקום כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle of lifting for another does not apply when the lifter had no intention to acquire anything for the other person, as the act is merely considered taking possession on behalf of a creditor.

rashi.hebrew: והכא - דקתני במתניתין שנים שהגביהו מציאה קנו מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that in the Mishnah's case of two people lifting a found item, they acquire it for each other based on the principle that one who acquires for themselves also acquires for their partner.

steps.title: Proof from agency in theft
steps.stepSummary: רבה supports his principle with a case of agency in theft, showing that self-acquisition extends to partners.
steps.whatsHappening: רבה brings a proof from the laws of theft to support the principle of מגו. This is a setup line introducing a proof.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא notes that an agent sent to steal is exempt, but partners who steal together are liable. This implies that one partner's act of acquisition for himself also acquires for the other, supporting the מגו principle.

keyTerms.meaning: There is no agency for sin

steps.whatToRemember: The proof from שותפין שגנבו shows that self-acquisition can extend to others.
steps.whyThisMatters: It establishes the מגו principle as a working halakhic rule, not just a theoretical possibility.

phrases.english: Know that he said to his agent:
"Go out and steal for me"
and he stole – exempt.
And partners who stole – liable.
What is the reason? Is it not because we say:

phrases.aramaic: מִגּוֹ דְּזָכֵי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכֵי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ. שְׁמַע מִינַּהּ.
phrases.english: Since he acquires for himself, he acquires also for his partner. Learn from it.

steps.triggerLanguage: תִּדַּע שֶׁאִלּוּ אָמַר לִשְׁלוּחוֹ: ״צֵא וּגְנוֹב לִי״, וְגָנַב – פָּטוּר.

rashi.hebrew: תדע - דאפילו אמר לא קנה חבירו היכא דהגביה כולה לדעת חבירו הכא דהגביה לעצמו ולחבירו קני משום מגו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the proof from partners who steal demonstrates that when one partner lifts an item for himself and his partner, the principle of מגו allows him to acquire it for both, unlike a pure agent who cannot acquire for another.

rashi.hebrew: פטור - המשלח פטור מלשלם כפל דקי"ל אין שליח לדבר עבירה להתחייב שולחו אלא שליח דדברי הרב ודברי התלמיד דברי מי שומעין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the sender is exempt from double payment because there is no agency for sin, and the agent's words take precedence over the sender's in matters of transgression.

rashi.hebrew: ואילו שותפין שגנבו - והאחד הוציאה מרשות בעלים לדעתו ולדעת חבירו אמרינן בבבא קמא (דף עח:) דחייבין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י cites the ruling in בבא קמא that partners who steal are liable because one partner removed the item from the owner's domain for both of their intents.

tosafot.hebrew: ושותפין שגנבו - פי' בקונטרס שהוציא לדעתו ולדעת חבירו כדאיתא בב"ק (דף עח:) ואין נראה דהתם בטביחה איירי וטביחה אפי' אמר צא וטבח לי חייב דדרשינן (שם דף עא.) תחת לרבות השליח אלא שותפין שגנבו שהגביהו שניהם:
tosafot.english: Partners who stole [are liable]. Rava is demonstrating that there are areas of halacha where one person cannot be an agent for another, such as when a principal appoints an agent to steal in his behalf. It is clear that the principal/sender is not liable for the actions of the agent. However, if the agent is acting on behalf of himself as well as for the principal, the agency is effective. For example: Ruvain appoints Shimon to steal a sheep for Ruvain and invites Shimon to be a partner in the theft. Ruvain and Shimon are equally responsible for the stolen sheep. But how do we know this to be true? Perhaps even in such a case, Shimon’s theft is effective only on behalf of himself and he alone is responsible for the sheep? Rashi points to a Baraisa in Bava Kama (78b) that clearly says “partners who stole are liable”. Rashi understands that this Baraisa is proof that when Shimon steals a sheep for himself and for Ruvain, both are liable. Tosfos will examine Rashi’s conclusion and eventually disagree with him. For a better understanding of the difference of opinions, we will learn the Gemara in Bava Kama (78b) together: The Gemara there is discussing the liability of one who first steals sheep or cattle and then slaughters or sells it. The Torah says in Mishpatim (Shemos 21, 37): He shall pay five cattle in place of the cattle and four sheep in place of the sheep. The Gemara in Bava Kama (78b) is discussing the rule that one who slaughters the sheep is liable only when his action is totally forbidden. However, if he has the legal right to slaughter half the animal, even though he does not have the right to slaughter the his partner’s share, he is not liable at all for the special payment of four times the value of the sheep. Baraisa a): So too, one who steals an animal owned by partners is liable. Partners who stole an animal and then slaughtered it are exempt. Baraisa b): But we learned in a Baraisa: Partners who stole are liable? Rav Nachman says: There is no contradiction. Here, in Baraisa a) we are speaking of when partner a) stole from his fellow partner b) and then slaughtered the animal. Since he has the legal right to slaughter his own half of the animal, he is not held liable for slaughtering his partner’s half. One is liable only when slaughtering the animal is totally prohibited. Here, in Baraisa b) we are speaking of when a partner stole1When the Baraisa speaks of a partner a) who stole from anybody else we are saying that when partner a) steals, partner b) is equally liable for the theft and its ramifications such as liability for four or five times the sheep or cattle when slaughtering or selling the stolen animal. But how does one become liable for another’s theft? Rashi here ד'ה ואילו שותפין שגנבו says that it is speaking of when partner a) stole the sheep or cattle with the intention of acquiring it for partner b) as well. Thus we see from this Baraisa that even though partner a) could not acquire the sheep or cattle for partner b) when acting exclusively for partner b) because there is no agency for a prohibited action, even so when partner a) is acquiring the animal for himself as well, we say that since he is acquiring it for himself he can also acquire it for his fellow partner b). from anybody else. Since, he has no right to slaughter any part of the stolen animal, his slaughtering is totally forbidden and he is liable for the penalty of four times the sheep’s value. Rava challenged Rav Nachman’s reconciliation of the two contradicting Baraisos: Baraisa c): It might be that a partner who stole from his fellow thief or partners who stole from anyone else are liable? [The Torah] therefore states: And he will slaughter it, which implies that for liability it is required that slaughtering the animal must be entirely illegal. In these cases slaughtering the animal is not entirely illegal and this requirement is lacking. Rav Nachman’s resolution of the contradiction between Baraisa a) and Baraisa b) is that: Baraisa a) which says that partners who stole are exempt is speaking of when partners stole from each other. Since partner a) who slaughters the animal is permitted to slaughter his half, there is no liability even for partner b)’s half. Baraisa b) which says that partners who stole are liable is speaking of when partners stole from anybody else. Regardless of which of the partners slaughters the animal, it is totally illegal, since it belongs entirely to a third party. However, in Baraisa c) we see that in addition to an exemption for partners who stole from each other, there is another case of partners who stole from anybody else and they are exempt. We must now find a resolution for Baraisos b) and c) in which both are not speaking of partners who stole from each other, but of partner’s who stole from elsewhere, and yet in Baraisa b) they are liable and in Baraisa c) they are exempt. Rather, Rav Nachman says there is no contradiction: Here in Baraisa b) which says that partners who stole from anybody else are liable we are speaking of when partner a) to this theft slaughtered the sheep with partner b)’s consent. He is acting in behalf of both partners and they are both liable. Here in Baraisa c) which says that partners who stole are exempt we are speaking of when partner a) slaughtered the animal stolen together without the consent of partner b). Insofar as one of the stolen halves is concerned partner a) would be liable because he has stolen and slaughtered, but he is exempt because he has not slaughtered an entire animal in a way that carries liability, for in regard to the second half of the animal it is as if partner b) stole the animal and partner a) slaughtered it, since partner a) slaughtered the animal without partner b)’s consent. When a) steals and b) slaughters an animal there is no liability for four times it value on either a) or b). Rashi explains: When our Gemara speaks of partners who stole, it is speaking of when partner a) carried the animal out of the victim’s domain for himself and for his partner b), as in Bava Kama (78b), where the Baraisa speaks of partner a) stealing an animal and the animal is considered a partnership theft with both partners sharing liability for the subsequent slaughtering or selling of the animal. [Rashi’s explanation] is not appealing. For there (Bava Kama 78b), [the Baraisa] is speaking about liability for four or five times the value of the animal for slaughtering2Tosfos understood that Rashi is referring to the liability for both partners when partner a) slaughters the animal. If so, his challenge to Rashi is correct. A ruling that both are liable for partner a)’s slaughtering the animal could be a result of the fact that agency is effective for slaughtering an animal and results in liability for the principal. However, see קצות חושן (348, 3), אילת השחר, נחלת דוד and משנת הלוי who argue that Rashi’s proof is not from the slaughtering of the animal, but rather from the fact that the Gemara there speaks of ‘partners who stole’. The Baraisa is saying that there is a situation where we consider partner a) and b) as partners in the act of stealing, even before there ever was an act of slaughtering. It is in reference to this act that Rashi says, it came about because partner a) stole the animal for himself and his partner. it. And in regard to slaughtering the animal, even if one said to his agent go and slaughter this animal for me, [the principal] is liable even though we usually rule there is no agency for a prohibited act, because we expound the superfluous word ‘תחת - in place of’ in the verse ‘five cattle in place of the ox’ to include an agent, who can act in behalf of the principal in this forbidden action of slaughtering a stolen animal. It is for this reason that when partner a) slaughters an animal stolen by himself and partner b) that both are liable. There is absolutely no proof from this case that when partner a) steals for himself and partner b), that both are liable. Rather, the term partners who stole is speaking of when both partner a) and partner b) lifted the stolen item together.3See קצות חושן ibid. who wonders why Tosfos insists that the Baraisa in Bava Kama (78b) is speaking of when both partners lifted the animal together. Even if so, acquiring the animal is dependent on the logic ‘since he can acquire for himself, he can also acquire for his fellow’. Otherwise, we would have to view the second half of the stolen article as if no act of acquisition was performed upon it, as is evident in our Gemara. If so, why must Tosfos insist that it is speaking only of when both partners lifted the animal, the same logic should apply even when only partner a) lifts the animal himself. He is acquiring it for himself and partner b) and they both are partners in the theft. Rashi on the other hand is saying that the Baraisa in Bava Kama (78b) is speaking of when only one partner stole the animal for the benefit of both, says so because that is a situation which parallels our Gemara which speaks about one lifting a found object for himself and his fellow. Furthermore, the Gemara there in its initial resolution of the contradiction between Baraisos a) and b), uses the expression ‘כאן בשותף שגנב מעלמא’ which literally translates as ‘one partner stealing from anybody else’. It seems that the Gemara is clearly sneaking of when ‘partner a) stole for himself and for the benefit of partner c).
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות challenges רש״י's understanding of the source in בבא קמא, arguing that the liability of partners there refers to slaughter (where agency is permitted) or requires both partners to physically lift the item, rather than one lifting for both via מגו.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות disputes רש״י's derivation of the מגו principle from the law of partners in theft, arguing that the source in בבא קמא does not support the idea that one partner's act of acquisition automatically covers the other via מגו.

steps.title: רבה's application to a deaf-mute and a competent person
steps.stepSummary: רבה applies the מגו principle to a case of a deaf-mute and a competent person lifting a found object.
steps.whatsHappening: רבה extends the מגו principle to a new scenario involving a deaf-mute and a competent person. This is a transition line introducing a new application.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבה argues that since the deaf-mute acquires his share, the competent person also acquires his share through the מגו principle. This sets up a test case for the principle's limits.

keyTerms.meaning: A deaf-mute and a mentally competent person
Since the deaf-mute acquires, the competent person also acquires

steps.whyThisMatters: It pushes the מגו principle to its limits, testing whether it works when one party lacks legal capacity.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא, הַשְׁתָּא דְּאָמְרַתְּ אָמְרִינַן מִגּוֹ:
phrases.english: Rava said: Now that you say we say from [miggo]:
A deaf-mute and a mentally competent person who lifted a found object,
From the fact that the deaf-mute acquired – the mentally competent person acquired.
phrases.notes: Refers to the legal principle of 'miggo' (since he could have claimed X, his claim of Y is believed), applied here to acquisition.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא, הַשְׁתָּא דְּאָמְרַתְּ אָמְרִינַן מִגּוֹ: חֵרֵשׁ וּפִקֵּחַ שֶׁהִגְבִּיהוּ מְצִיאָה, מִתּוֹךְ שֶׁקָּנָה חֵרֵשׁ – קָנָה פִּקֵּחַ.

rashi.hebrew: השתא דאמרת מגו - דזכי לנפשיה כו' חרש ופקח שהגביהו מציאה קנאוה אע"פ שהגבהה של חרש אינה הגבהה לקנות אלא מפני דרכי שלום כדתנן (גיטין דף נט:) חרש שוטה וקטן יש בהן גזל מפני דרכי שלום אפילו הכי אמר מגו דזכי לנפשיה כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that although the deaf-mute's act of lifting does not constitute a valid קניין, it is effective for the sake of דרכי שלום, and therefore the מגו principle allows the competent person to acquire his share as well.

steps.title: Challenge to the competent person's acquisition
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges how the competent person acquires, since the deaf-mute cannot acquire for others.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא challenges רבה's ruling by asking how the פיקח acquires the item. This is a direct קושיא on the previous resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The חרש acquires because the פיקח lifts for him, but the פיקח cannot acquire through the חרש since a חרש lacks legal agency. This circular dependency breaks the logic of the previous תירוץ.

keyTerms.meaning: How does the competent person acquire?

phrases.english: In any case, the deaf-mute acquired
For the mentally competent person lifted it for him.
But the mentally competent person, in what did he acquire?

steps.triggerLanguage: בִּשְׁלָמָא חֵרֵשׁ קָנָה – דְּקָא מַגְבַּהּ לֵיהּ בֶּן דַּעַת. אֶלָּא פִּקֵּחַ בְּמַאי קָנָה?

rashi.hebrew: בשלמא חרש קני דקא מגבה לה בן דעת - לראשה השני לצורך שניהם וראשו של צד חרש לגבי חרש מיהא הויא הגבהה כדתקון ליה מפני דרכי שלום אלא פקח במאי קני לגבי פקח הוי ראשה שהגביה החרש כמונח על גבי קרקע דהגבהה דחרש לא קניא אלא מדרכי שלום בעלמא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the חרש acquires because the בן דעת lifts the item for both parties, and the חרש's side of the item is considered lifted for him due to the enactment of דרכי שלום, whereas for the פקח, the חרש's lifting is considered as if the item remains on the ground since the חרש's act of lifting does not constitute a valid קניין except by way of דרכי שלום.

steps.title: Resolution: Only the deaf-mute acquires
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that only the deaf-mute acquires, and the competent person does not.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the challenge by limiting the acquisition to the חרש alone. This is a תירוץ that abandons the פיקח's acquisition.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The logic shifts: since two חרשין acquire together, this single חרש also acquires. The פיקח does not acquire, which avoids the agency problem entirely.

keyTerms.meaning: Since two deaf-mutes in the world acquire

phrases.english: Rather, say: Deaf-mute –
acquires, mentally competent –
does not acquire. And what is the miggo?

phrases.aramaic: מִגּוֹ דִּשְׁנֵי חֵרְשִׁין בְּעָלְמָא קָנוּ, הַאי נָמֵי קָנֵי.
phrases.english: From the fact that two deaf-mutes in the world acquire, this one too acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אֵימָא: חֵרֵשׁ – קָנָה, פִּקֵּחַ – לָא קָנָה.

rashi.hebrew: ומאי מגו - איכא למימר הכא דקאמר השתא דאמרת מגו אמרינן מגו וחרש אמאי קני הא לא זכי פקח לנפשיה דנימא דזכי נמי לחבריה ולא הויא הגבהה דפקח הגבהה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the principle of מגו here means that since the deaf-mute acquires for himself, he can also acquire for his partner, thereby validating the act of lifting (הגבהה) for the competent person as well.

tosafot.hebrew: ומאי מגו מגו דשני חרשין - תימה מנלן דלמא תרי מגו לא אמרי' דב' חרשין דקנו היינו מטעם מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה:
tosafot.english: And what is the “since” [principle that allows the deaf-mute to acquire the found object, even when the competent person does not acquire his half of the found object?] “Since” two deaf-mutes [would both acquire this found object, we view the competent person as if he is a deaf-mute and this allows his deaf-mute partner to acquire half of the cloak.] Rami bar Chama and Rava agree that understanding our Mishna requires some special principle. For if we were to apply our previous understanding to the case of the Mishna, we would rule that neither of the litigants acquires the found object, because we would view the section of the garment in Ruvain’s hands as if it is on the ground in regard to Shimon and the section in Shimon’s hands as if it is on the ground in regard to Ruvain acquiring his half of the found object. Since neither has lifted the entire cloak from the ground, they both do not acquire it. We are compelled to say that each is lifting the cloak for himself and for his partner. Rami bar Chama concluded that our Mishna proves that one can act in behalf of his fellow when lifting a found object. Rava disagrees. He maintains that the ruling of the Mishna is limited to when Ruvain is lifting the cloak for himself and for Shimon and Shimon is also lifting the cloak for himself and for Ruvain. However, if Ruvain would lift only for Shimon, he could not acquire it for Shimon. We only say: Since Ruvain is acquiring the cloak for himself, he can also acquire it for Shimon, but he cannot acquire it solely for Shimon. Once this “since” principle has been established, Rava takes it to the next level. Concerning a situation where Ruvain was a competent person and Shimon was a deaf-mute, he says that Shimon, the deaf-mute cannot assist Ruvain in acquiring his half, but Ruvain can assist Shimon, the deaf-mute, in acquiring his half. But if Ruvain is not acquiring any of the cloak, how can he act in behalf of Shimon, the deaf-mute? Rava answers: “Since” if Shimon’s partner was also a deaf-mute, Shimon would acquire at least half, so too, even when Shimon’s partner is a competent person, Shimon acquires half the cloak. (See Artscroll note 20.) Rava in his second ruling is expanding the “since” principle to a second level. Tosfos questions the legitimacy of this approach. Let us remember that the original “since” principle is accepted only because it is manifest in the Mishna. Rava insisted that we cannot accept the principle that one who lifts a found object for his fellow acquires it for his fellow, because that is not evident from the Mishna. The Mishna only proves that Ruvain and Shimon can act for each other only when acquiring for themselves as well. If we are limited to what is evident from the Mishna, Tosfos wonders how Rava can expand the “since” principle to a second level. This is bewildering! How do we know that we can expand the “since” principle to include Ruvain, the competent person, acting in behalf of Shimon, the deaf-mute? Perhaps we do not say two “migos”? But why must we concede that we are dealing with an expanded level of the “since” principle? For the ruling that two deaf-mutes can acquire is because of the “since” principle, “since” each of the deaf-mutes is acquiring for himself, he may acquire for his fellow. If not for this first application of the “since” principle, we would rule that we view each end of the cloak as if it is on the ground. It is only because of the “since” principle that we say, each can acquire for his fellow when acquiring for himself. The next application of the “since” principle, since two deaf-mutes can acquire for each other etc. is a second level of the “since” principle and we have no proof from the Mishna that we can move to a second level.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions the Gemara's use of מגו, asking why we assume two deaf-mutes acquire based on מגו, when perhaps the rule of two deaf-mutes acquiring is independent and does not rely on the principle that one who acquires for himself acquires for another.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות challenges the underlying assumption of the Gemara's resolution, questioning whether the acquisition by two deaf-mutes is actually derived from the same מגו principle that רש״י applies to the mixed case.

steps.title: Challenge to the deaf-mute's acquisition
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the deaf-mute's acquisition, arguing that the competent person's lack of acquisition should prevent the deaf-mute from acquiring.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא challenges this new resolution by questioning the חרש's acquisition. This is a second קושיא on the תירוץ.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the פיקח does not acquire, the lifting is for his own benefit, not for the חרש. Therefore, the חרש should not acquire either, as the act was not intended for him.

keyTerms.meaning: He lifts it for his own benefit
If he doesn't acquire, does he acquire for others?

phrases.english: What is this?

phrases.aramaic: אִם תִּמְצָא לוֹמַר הַמַּגְבִּיהַּ מְצִיאָה לַחֲבֵירוֹ קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ,
phrases.english: If you say that one who lifts a found item for his friend acquires it for his friend,

phrases.aramaic: הָנֵי מִילֵּי הֵיכָא דְּקָא מַגְבַּהּ לֵיהּ אַדַּעְתָּא דְּחַבְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: These matters are where he is lifting it for him with the awareness of his friend.

phrases.aramaic: הַאי אַדַּעְתָּא דִּידֵיהּ קָא מַגְבַּהּ לֵיהּ,
phrases.english: This one is lifting it for him with his own awareness,
He does not acquire –
For others he acquires?!

steps.triggerLanguage: הַאי מַאי? אִם תִּמְצָא לוֹמַר הַמַּגְבִּיהַּ מְצִיאָה לַחֲבֵירוֹ קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ, הָנֵי מִילֵּי הֵיכָא דְּקָא מַגְבַּהּ לֵיהּ אַדַּעְתָּא דְּחַבְרֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: אם תמצא לומר - כלומר אפילו אם תמצא לומר המגביה מציאה לחבירו כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's phrase 'אם תמצא לומר' introduces a hypothetical concession that even if one accepts the principle that lifting a מציאה for another acquires it for them, this only applies when the lifter acts with the other person's awareness.

rashi.hebrew: הכא אדעתא דידיה קא מגבה ליה - האי פיקח:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that in this case, the פיקח is lifting the item for his own benefit, not for the חביר, which is why the acquisition logic fails.

steps.title: Resolution: Neither acquires
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that neither the deaf-mute nor the competent person acquires.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a final resolution that neither party acquires the item. This is a תירוץ that abandons both acquisitions.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Since the פיקח does not acquire, the חרש also does not acquire. This resolves the circular dependency by rejecting the acquisition entirely in this mixed case.

keyTerms.term: מִתּוֹךְ שֶׁלֹּא קָנָה פִּקַּח – לֹא קָנָה חֵרֵשׁ
keyTerms.meaning: Since the competent person doesn't acquire, the deaf-mute also doesn't acquire

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא אֵימָא: מִתּוֹךְ שֶׁלֹּא קָנָה פִּקֵּחַ –
phrases.english: Rather, say: Since the mentally competent person did not acquire
the deaf-mute did not acquire.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אֵימָא: מִתּוֹךְ שֶׁלֹּא קָנָה פִּקֵּחַ – לֹא קָנָה חֵרֵשׁ.

steps.title: Resolution: The Sages' enactment for two deaf-mutes
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that the Sages enacted that two deaf-mutes acquire to prevent disputes, but this doesn't apply here.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains why two חרשין acquire, distinguishing it from the current case. This is a תירוץ that clarifies the basis of the previous rule.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The רבנן enacted that two חרשין acquire to prevent disputes. In the current case, the חרש knows the פיקח doesn't acquire, so he won't dispute with others, making the enactment unnecessary.

keyTerms.meaning: So that they won't come to dispute

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי תֵּימָא, מַאי שְׁנָא מִשְּׁנֵי חֵרְשִׁין דְּעָלְמָא –
phrases.english: And if you would say: What is the difference from two deaf-mutes of the world?

phrases.aramaic: הָתָם תַּקִּינוּ לְהוּ רַבָּנַן דְּלָא אָתֵי לְאִנְּצוֹיֵי.
phrases.english: There the Sages instituted for them that they not come to quarrel.

phrases.aramaic: הָכָא מֵימָר אָמַר: פִּקֵּחַ לָא קָנֵי, אֲנָא אֶקְנֵי?
phrases.english: Here the deaf-mute says: A mentally competent person does not acquire, I will acquire?

steps.triggerLanguage: וְכִי תֵּימָא, מַאי שְׁנָא מִשְּׁנֵי חֵרְשִׁין דְּעָלְמָא – הָתָם תַּקִּינוּ לְהוּ רַבָּנַן דְּלָא אָתֵי לְאִנְּצוֹיֵי.

rashi.hebrew: דלא ליתי לאינצויי - עם החוטפים מהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the enactment for two deaf-mutes was made to prevent others from snatching the item from them.

steps.title: רבה אחא's question about the source of רמי בר חמא's derivation
steps.stepSummary: רבה אחא asks רבה אשי where רמי בר חמא derives his principle from the Mishnah.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא shifts to a new question about the source of רמי בר חמא's derivation. This is a setup for a new discussion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רבה אחא asks whether רמי בר חמא derives his principle from the first clause of the משנה, where each person claims the whole garment. This sets up a search for the correct source.

keyTerms.meaning: The precise derivation of רמי בר חמא

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב
phrases.english: Rav Aḥa, son of Rav Adda, said to Rav Ashi:
From where is Rami bar Ḥama’s inference drawn?
If we say from the beginning, 'Two holding a garment,'
there this one says, 'All of it is mine, and I lifted it all,'

phrases.aramaic: הָתָם הַאי קָאָמַר ״כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וַאֲנָא אַגְּבַּהְתַּהּ כּוּלַּהּ״,
phrases.english: and this one says, 'All of it is mine, and I lifted it all.'

phrases.aramaic: וְהַאי אָמַר ״כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וַאֲנָא אַגְּבַּהְתַּהּ כּוּלַּהּ״.
phrases.english: How can the halakha where one acquires an item for another be inferred from that case?

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב אַדָּא לְרַב אָשֵׁי: דִּיּוּקֵיהּ דְּרָמֵי בַּר חָמָא מֵהֵיכָא?

rashi.hebrew: מהיכא - מאיזו בבא ממשנתנו קא דייק לומר זאת אומרת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה אחא is asking from which specific clause in the Mishnah רמי בר חמא derives his principle.

rashi.hebrew: הא קאמר כולה שלי ואנא אגבהתיה - והיאך נאמר לא יקנה לא זה ולא זה ויבא אחר ויטלנה והלא כל אחד טוען אני הגבהתיה ואין כאן מגביה מציאה לחבירו ומה יש לו להשיב בשלמא האי שכנגדו המוחזק בה כמוהו אמר לא הגבהת אתה כי אם אני אבל אחר מן השוק מה יטעון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the derivation were from the first clause where both claim they lifted the garment, it would be impossible to rule that neither acquires it and a third party takes it, because each claims to have lifted it and there is no one else who lifted a מציאה for the other, unlike a case where the other party is holding it and can counterclaim.

steps.title: Answer: The derivation is from the superfluous clause
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara answers that רמי בר חמא derives his principle from a superfluous clause in the Mishnah.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers that רמי בר חמא derives his principle from a superfluous clause in the משנה. This is a תשובה to the previous question.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The clause where both claim the whole garment is superfluous if the first clause already covers the case. This superfluous clause teaches that lifting for another acquires.

keyTerms.meaning: He learns from the superfluous Mishnaic clause

phrases.english: But from that which it says: "This one says all of it

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁלִּי וְזֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי״ הָא
phrases.english: is mine, and this one says all of it is mine" — this
then, what do I need it for?
Rather, from a superfluous Mishnah he learns from it:
One who lifts a found object for his friend — his friend acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מֵהָא דְּקָתָנֵי ״זֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וְזֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי״ הָא תּוּ לְמָה לִי?

rashi.hebrew: ממשנה יתירה - אמרינן דהמגביה מציאה כו' ומשום הכי אמרינן יחלוקו דאמרינן שניהם הגביהוה והוה ליה כל אחד מגביה מציאה לו ולחבירו וקני דאי משום דלא ידעינן הי מינייהו משקר והוה ליה ממון המוטל בספק וחולקין בשבועה משום שלא יהא כל אחד ואחד הולך ותוקף כדאמרי' לעיל (בבא מציעא דף ג.). הא שמענא ליה מרישא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the superfluous clause teaches that when both parties lift the garment, each is considered to have lifted a מציאה for himself and his partner, thereby acquiring it, which prevents the case from being treated as ממון המוטל בספק requiring a שבועה to avoid each party seizing the item.

steps.title: Challenge: The first clause is מציאה and the second is מקח וממכר
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous answer, arguing that the first clause is about מציאה and the second about מקח וממכר.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא objects to the previous answer by distinguishing the two clauses of the משנה. This is a קושיא on the תירוץ.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first clause deals with מציאה, while the second deals with מקח וממכר. This means the second clause is not superfluous, as it teaches a new case, undermining the derivation.

keyTerms.meaning: The first clause is about a found object
The latter clause is about a purchase

phrases.aramaic: וְהָא אוֹקֵימְנָא רֵישָׁא בִּמְצִיאָה, וְסֵיפָא בְּמִקָּח וּמִמְכָּר!
phrases.english: And didn't we establish the beginning in a found item, and the end in a purchase and…

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָא אוֹקֵימְנָא רֵישָׁא בִּמְצִיאָה, וְסֵיפָא בְּמִקָּח וּמִמְכָּר!

steps.title: Resolution: The derivation is from the clause about half ownership
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that רמי בר חמא derives his principle from the clause where one claims half ownership.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the previous challenge by pinpointing a different source for רמי בר חמא's derivation. It identifies the clause where one claims the whole garment and the other claims half as the key.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This clause is superfluous if earlier cases already cover the scenario. Therefore, it must teach that lifting for another acquires.

keyTerms.term: זֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וְזֶה אוֹמֵר חֶצְיָהּ שֶׁלִּי
keyTerms.meaning: One says it's all mine, the other says half is mine

steps.whatToRemember: The derivation is from the clause about half ownership.

phrases.english: But from the latter clause:

phrases.aramaic: ״זֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וְזֶה אוֹמֵר חֶצְיָהּ שֶׁלִּי״,
phrases.english: "This one says all of it is mine and this one says half of it is mine"
Here too, what do I need it for?
But from a superfluous Mishnah, he heard from it:
One who lifts a found object for his friend — his friend acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מִסֵּיפָא: ״זֶה אוֹמֵר כּוּלָּהּ שֶׁלִּי וְזֶה אוֹמֵר חֶצְיָהּ שֶׁלִּי״, הָא תּוּ לְמָה לִי?

tosafot.hebrew: אלא מסיפא זה אומר כולה שלי וזה אומר חציה שלי - אית דל"ג הכא הא תו למה לי דממשנה גופה קא דייק דקאמר חציה שלי והיינו שהגביה עם חבירו ולפי דבריו לא יטול כלום אלא ודאי מגביה מציאה לחבירו זכה ופריך ממאי דבמציאה דלמא במקח וממכר והוא הדין דהוה מצי למדחי דהא דאמר חציה שלי כגון דאמר תפסתיה חציה בידי או הגבהתיה כולה לקנות חציה אבל עדיין היה מדקדק מיתורא דמתני' לכך דחי ליה דאפי' מיתורא אינו יכול לדקדק ולספרים דגרסי הא תו למה לי י"ל דמשום דמגופא דמתני' לא מצי למידק כדפרישית אך קשה אמאי קאמר דלמא במקח וממכר אפילו מיירי במציאה מצי לדחויי כדדחי וי"ל בדוחק ממאי דבמציאה דלהכי לא מוקמת במקח וממכר משום דלא משכחת חידוש אלא במגביה מציאה לחבירו כי נמי איירי במקח וממכר יש חידוש דלא נימא משיב אבידה הוא וה"ה אם מיירי במציאה לא יוכל להוכיח כלום:
tosafot.english: Rather, [Rami bar Chama’s principle can be derived] from the end [of the first paragraph of our Mishna, which reads]: This [litigant] says it is all mine and this [litigant] says half is mine. Tosfos will discuss two variant texts of our Gemara. We will refer to the text as it is printed in our Gemara as text a) and to the variant text as text b). Rami bar Chama says that our Mishna proves his principle: One who lifts up a found object for his fellow acquires it for him. We find two methods of deducing a legal principle from a Mishna or Baraisa. A) The simplest way is that the ruling of the Mishna makes sense only if the principle we are attempting to prove is true. B) Redundancy. The particular ruling of the Mishna may not necessarily prove that the principle is true. There may be another way of understanding the Mishna, which would not prove the principle in question. However, if the Tanna repeated an unnecessary case, we may assume that his purpose was to teach us this principle. Of course the principle must be a reasonable explanation of the ruling, even if not compelling. The Gemara refers to this method as ‘משנה יתירה - a redundant Mishna’. The major difference between text a) and text b) is whether the Gemara is attempting to prove Rami bar Chama’s principle from the redundancy of a seemingly unneeded case in the Mishna (text a) or from the ruling of the Mishna (text b). Text a) the Gemara continues to demonstrate how we can see Rami bar Chama’s principle from our Mishna: Why was it necessary for the Mishna to say more? The underlying principles governing the ruling in the second case, that each receives half of what they are arguing about can be seen from the first case. See Rashi (2a) ד'ה וזה אומר חציה שלי, also Rashi here ד'ה ממשנה יתירה. Rather, from the redundancy of this Mishna, we learn that one who lifts up a found object for his fellow, has acquired it for his fellow. The Gemara rejects this proof: Why are you certain that this case in the Mishna is speaking about a found object, perhaps it is speaking of a sale item? The ruling of the Mishna would then have nothing to do with lifting up a found object for one’s fellow. If you counter: If we are speaking of a sale item, what is the Mishna teaching us? It is needed. I might think: This [litigant] who claims half is mine should be thought of as one who is returning a lost object, since he could have just as easily claimed it is all mine, and he should be exempt from taking an oath, [the Mishna] teaches us even so he must swear. When analyzing the dialogue of the Gemara we are faced with a severe difficulty. When the Gemara deflects the proof from this case of the Mishna it suggests: Perhaps it is speaking of purchasing and selling? The Gemara then goes on to explain that the need for this case is to teach us that we do not consider the litigant who claims only half as one who is returning a lost object and he is still required to swear. But this lesson is needed even in the case of a found object? There too, we might think that the litigant who claims half is mine should be exempt from swearing. Why did the Gemara first say that the Mishna is speaking of a sale item? In the course of analyzing texts a) and b), Tosfos will show how we might deal with this difficulty. Text b): There are those who at this point in the Gemara’s dialogue do not have the text: Why was it necessary for the Mishna to say any more? This leading sentence tells us that by (method A) studying the Mishna’s ruling that the litigant who claims it is all mine receives three quarters and his opponent who claims half receives one quarter, we could not reach the conclusion that one who lifts a found object for his fellow acquires it for his fellow. It is only because the ruling is redundant (method B) that we say the Tanna intended to teach us this principle. But, Tosfos argues, in this section of the Mishna, we could derive Rami bar Chama’s principle from (method A) the very ruling that one of the litigants receives a quarter of the cloak! For it is from the very ruling of the Mishna, not its redundancy, that [the Gemara] deduces that one who lifts up a found object in behalf of his fellow, his fellow has acquired it. Because [the litigant] says: Half is mine. This implies that it was acquired in a way that half belongs to each of the litigants. How can this be accomplished? This must mean that he lifted it together with his fellow litigant, with each of them intending to acquire it for himself and for his fellow. If lifting for one’s fellow is ineffective, then according to his own claim he has acquired nothing. As explained earlier in the Gemara, when lifting the found object each of the litigants can acquire some part of it only if both are lifting the cloak for each other as well as for themselves. Since we honor his claim by awarding him a quarter of the cloak, we are ruling that lifting the found object for one’s fellow is effective. Otherwise, he would own nothing of the cloak. Rather, it is certain that one who lifts a found object acquires it for his fellow. Thus he has assisted his fellow in acquiring half the cloak, just as the fellow has assisted him in acquiring his share of the cloak. Tosfos explains how according to text b) the Gemara deflects this proof: [The Gemara] challenges this proof by arguing: How do you know the Mishna is speaking about a found object which would prove that one can lift up a found object for his fellow? Perhaps [the Mishna] is speaking about two litigants who claim that they acquired this cloak through purchasing and selling. Lifting an object for the benefit of another person is problematic only when lifting a found object. This found object can be acquired by anybody. Taking it for one’s fellow is detrimental to the interest of all other people who might want to acquire it. Perhaps one cannot act in behalf of his fellow when his action is detrimental to others? This does not apply to a sale where the seller has the right to choose to whom he wishes to sell it. Certainly, anybody may lift a sale object in behalf of the intended purchaser. There are two other possible interpretations of our Mishna that would yield no light on whether one may acquire a found object for his fellow. It is conceivable that one of the litigants could have acquired half the cloak without the assistance of his fellow. So too, [the Gemara] could have deflected the proof from this second case of the Mishna by saying that [the litigant] who claims “half is mine” is a situation where he says: I grasped half in my hand. We learned earlier (7a) that any portion of the cloak that is actually in the hand of a litigant belongs to that litigant and is not subject to division. It is only the portion suspended between the two litigants that is subject to division. Thus, if one litigant claims that he grasped half the cloak in his hands, that portion is his without the benefit of his fellow-litigant lifting the other half for him. The claim “half is mine”, does not necessarily mean that the litigants lifted it up in a way where each of them needs his fellow litigant to assist him in acquiring it. The Mishna may very well be speaking of a case where one can acquire half the cloak without the benefit of his fellow’s assistance. The second: or he might claim I lifted the entire cloak with the intention of acquiring only half. One can certainly acquire only half of the cloak, if that is his wish. It is therefore conceivable that the claim “half is mine” does not necessarily mean that I lifted the cloak in a manner that requires my fellow-litigant’s assistance to acquire it. It can also mean I lifted the entire cloak myself, which is a perfect act of acquisition, with the intention of acquiring only half. Tosfos demonstrated that there are three ways of understanding the claim “half is mine”, which would not reflect on whether one who lifts a found object for his fellow has acquired it for his fellow. a) The answer that the Gemara uses: The Mishna may be speaking not of a found object, but of a sale item. b) The Mishna may be speaking of a litigant who claims that he acquired half the cloak by grasping it in his hands. c) The Mishna may be speaking of when the litigant claims he lifted the entire cloak, but only with the intention of acquiring half. Obviously, with all these available interpretations of the Mishna, we cannot deduce from the Mishna per se, that one who lifts a found object for his fellow has acquired it for him, but even though it is not evident from (method A) the ruling of the Mishna, [the Gemara] could still have deduced Rami bar Chama’s principle from (method B) the redundancy of the Mishna. Therefore, [the Gemara] deflects [the proof from this case in our Mishna] in such a way that even from the redundancy we cannot deduce that one who lifts a found object for his fellow has acquired it for him. The Gemara says that this case is in fact needed to teach us that the litigant who claims half is mine must also take an oath. If so, there is no redundancy to teach Rami bar Chama’s principle. Tosfos now explains text a). First, Tosfos explains the strength of text a): Concerning those manuscripts who do have the text: “Why was it necessary to say any more?”, which indicates that it is only from (method B) the redundancy, that we can deduce Rami bar Chama’s principle, it can be said that this is because [the Gemara] could not have deduced this from the Mishna itself since there are three other ways to understand this case of the Mishna as we explained. Thus, an attempt to prove Rami bar Chama’s principle from the ruling of the Mishna is inherently weak. The Gemara was proving his principle from the redundancy of this case. Tosfos now explains the weakness of text a): However, there is a difficulty with text a). Why does [the Gemara] say: Perhaps [the Mishna] is speaking about purchasing and selling and there is no discussion at all about lifting a found object for one’s fellow. Since the Gemara will argue that the Mishna is needed to teach us that even one who claims half must swear, even if [the Mishna] is speaking about a found object, we can deflect the proof from the Mishna as it is deflected when speaking about buying and selling. We can say that the Mishna is teaching us that in a case of a found object, when a litigant claims half is mine he still must swear. That novelty is equally applicable to a lost object as it is to buying and selling. Tosfos is convinced that any answer is not as good as the question. We can answer even if it is a bit awkward. The dialogue of the Gemara according to text a) is as follows: The deflector is arguing: Why are you so sure that the Mishna is speaking about a found object and thus you are convinced that the Tanna’s intent must be to teach us through (method B) redundancy, that one who lifts up a found object for his fellow acquires it for him? It is most probably for this reason that you do not interpret the Mishna as speaking about buying and selling: because you cannot find any novelty in this ruling other then if it is teaching us about one who lifts a found object for his fellow. However, in reality, even if it would be speaking about buying and selling there is a novelty: that we do not say that one who claims only half is thought of as returning a lost object and is therefore exempt from taking an oath. Now that the deflector demonstrated that there is a novelty even if the Mishna is speaking about buying and selling, we can move a step further and say: The same is true if the Mishna] is speaking about a found object. The Mishna is teaching us that in the case of a found object a litigant who claims half is mine is not thought of as returning a lost object and must swear. There are three steps to the deflector’s approach. 1) He first takes us all the way to the opposite end of the spectrum, buying and selling, where it seems least likely to find a novelty in the Mishna. 2) He shows us that even in that extreme case there is a novelty. 3) We can then adopt that same novelty and apply it to a found object.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara's derivation from the clause 'זה אומר כולה שלי וזה אומר חציה שלי' relies on the principle of משנה יתירה, as the ruling in this case is redundant if the previous cases already cover the scenario, thus teaching that lifting a מציאה for another acquires it for them.

steps.title: Challenge: The clause might be about מקח וממכר
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous resolution, arguing that the clause might be about a purchase, not a found object.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא objects to the previous resolution by offering an alternative explanation for the clause. It suggests the clause might be about a purchase, not a found object.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If it is about a purchase, the clause is not superfluous. It teaches a new case, undermining the derivation from a superfluous clause.

phrases.english: And from where that it is about a found item?

steps.triggerLanguage: וּמִמַּאי דְּבִמְצִיאָה? דִּלְמָא בְּמִקָּח וּמִמְכָּר?

steps.title: Resolution: The clause teaches about artifice in claiming half
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that the clause teaches about the possibility of artifice in claiming half ownership.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the challenge by explaining the purpose of the clause. It teaches that someone claiming half might be using artifice to avoid an oath.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Claiming the whole would require an oath. Claiming half makes it look like returning a lost item, avoiding the oath. This explains why the clause is necessary, even if it is about a purchase.

keyTerms.meaning: Like one returning a lost item

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי תֵּימָא: אִי בְּמִקָּח וּמִמְכָּר מַאי לְמֵימְרָא?
phrases.english: And if you would say: If in a case of buying and selling, what is there to…

phrases.aramaic: אִיצְטְרִיךְ, סָלְקָא דַּעְתָּךְ אָמֵינָא הַאי דְּקָאָמַר ״חֶצְיָהּ
phrases.english: It is necessary, lest you think that the one who says "half of it
is mine" should be considered as one returning a lost item and be exempt.

phrases.aramaic: קָמַשְׁמַע לַן דְּהַאי אִיעָרוֹמֵי קָא מַעֲרִים.
phrases.english: It teaches us that this one is employing artifice.
He thinks: If I say "all of it is mine",
I will need to take an oath, I will say this,
that I will be considered as one returning a lost item and I will be…

steps.triggerLanguage: וְכִי תֵּימָא: אִי בְּמִקָּח וּמִמְכָּר מַאי לְמֵימְרָא? אִיצְטְרִיךְ, סָלְקָא דַּעְתָּךְ אָמֵינָא הַאי דְּקָאָמַר ״חֶצְיָהּ שֶׁלִּי״ לֶהֱוֵי כְּמֵשִׁיב אֲבֵידָה וְלִיפְּטַר.

rashi.hebrew: דאהוי כמשיב אבידה - מדהוה מצי למימר כולה שלי ואמר חציה שלי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the claimant's statement of 'חציה שלי' is treated as returning a lost item because he could have claimed the entire item but chose not to.

rashi.hebrew: וליפטר - משבועה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that by being treated as one who returns a lost item, the claimant is exempt from taking a שבועה.

steps.title: Resolution: The derivation is from the clause about two riders
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that רמי בר חמא derives his principle from the clause about two riders on an animal.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a final resolution to the challenge by identifying a different source for רמי בר חמא's derivation. It points to the clause about two riders on an animal.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This clause is superfluous if earlier cases already cover the scenario. Therefore, it teaches that lifting for another acquires.

keyTerms.term: הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה
keyTerms.meaning: Two were riding on an animal

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא מֵהָא, הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה,
phrases.english: Rather, from this: If two were riding on an animal,
What do I need this for?
Rather, from a superfluous Mishnah we learn from it:
One who lifts a found item for his friend — his friend acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מֵהָא, הָיוּ שְׁנַיִם רוֹכְבִין עַל גַּבֵּי בְּהֵמָה, הָא תּוּ לְמָה לִי?

rashi.hebrew: ממשנה יתירה כו' - כדפרישית:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the derivation from a מִשְׁנָה יְתֵירָה is as he previously explained.

steps.title: Challenge: The clause teaches that riding acquires
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the previous resolution, arguing that the clause teaches that riding acquires, not lifting for another.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא objects to the previous resolution by offering an alternative explanation for the clause. It suggests the clause teaches that riding acquires, not that lifting for another acquires.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If it teaches that riding acquires, the clause is not superfluous. It teaches a new principle, undermining the derivation from a superfluous clause.

phrases.aramaic: וְדִלְמָא, הָא קָמַשְׁמַע לַן דְּרוֹכֵב נָמֵי קָנֵי!
phrases.english: And perhaps, this teaches us that one who sits also acquires!

steps.triggerLanguage: וְדִלְמָא, הָא קָמַשְׁמַע לַן דְּרוֹכֵב נָמֵי קָנֵי!

rashi.hebrew: דרכוב קני - ואע"ג דלא משיכה היא שאינה זזה ממקומה שאין מנהיגה ברגליו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's challenge is that the clause teaches that riding acquires, even though it is not משיכה since the animal does not move from its place by being led with the rider's feet.

steps.title: Resolution: The derivation is from the clause about admission or witnesses
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the challenge by stating that רמי בר חמא derives his principle from the clause about admission or witnesses.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא offers a final resolution to the challenge by identifying a different source for רמי בר חמא's derivation. It points to the clause about admission or witnesses.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This clause is superfluous if earlier cases already cover the scenario. Therefore, it teaches that lifting for another acquires. The גמרא argues that if it were about a purchase, it wouldn't need to be stated, so it must be about a found object.

keyTerms.term: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהֵן מוֹדִין אוֹ שֶׁיֵּשׁ לָהֶן עֵדִים
keyTerms.meaning: When they admit or have witnesses

phrases.english: Rather, from the latter clause:

phrases.aramaic: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהֵן מוֹדִין אוֹ שֶׁיֵּשׁ לָהֶן עֵדִים –
phrases.english: When they admit or when they have witnesses –
They divide without an oath. In what?
If in purchase and sale, is it necessary to say?
Rather, is it not in a found item?

phrases.aramaic: וּשְׁמַע מִינַּהּ הַמַּגְבִּיהַּ מְצִיאָה לַחֲבֵירוֹ קָנָה חֲבֵירוֹ.
phrases.english: And learn from it that one who lifts a found item for his friend, his friend acquires.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא מִסֵּיפָא: בִּזְמַן שֶׁהֵן מוֹדִין אוֹ שֶׁיֵּשׁ לָהֶן עֵדִים – חוֹלְקִין בְּלֹא שְׁבוּעָה.

rashi.hebrew: צריכא למימר - דהיכא דלקוחה בין שניהם שיחלקוה בלא שבועה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question 'צריכא למימר?' assumes the Mishna's clause about admission or witnesses is necessary to teach that when an item is purchased between two parties, they divide it without a שבועה.

rashi.hebrew: אלא לאו במציאה - ואיצטריך לאשמועינן שאין אחר יכול לחוטפה דמגביה מציאה לחבירו קנה חבירו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the derivation must be from מציאה, teaching that once one person lifts a found object for another, the other person acquires it and no one else can seize it.

steps.title: רבה's final principle: מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara concludes with רבה's principle that self-acquisition extends to others.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא concludes the sugya by reaffirming רבה's principle. It establishes that רבה's principle is the correct explanation for the משנה's case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This principle allows one to acquire for another when acquiring for oneself. It resolves the debate about רמי בר חמא's derivation.

keyTerms.term: מִגּוֹ דְּזָכֵי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכֵי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ
keyTerms.meaning: Since he acquires for himself, he also acquires for his partner

steps.whatToRemember: The final principle: מגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה.
steps.whyThisMatters: It establishes the מגו principle as the dominant view in this context.

phrases.english: And Rava says to you:

phrases.aramaic: מִגּוֹ דְּזָכֵי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכֵי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: From the fact that he acquires for himself, he acquires also for his partner.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְרָבָא אָמַר לָךְ: מִגּוֹ דְּזָכֵי לְנַפְשֵׁיהּ זָכֵי נָמֵי לְחַבְרֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: ורבא אמר לך - בעלמא היכא דלא נתכוין המגביה לזכות בה לא קנה חבירו ומשנה יתירה דמתני' אשמועינן המגביה מציאה לו ולחבירו קנה אף חבירו דמגו דזכי לנפשיה זכי נמי לחבריה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבא's principle is not the general rule, as normally one does not acquire for another without intent, but rather the Mishnah teaches a special case where acquiring for oneself also acquires for one's partner.

steps.title: Rav Yosef's report of Rav Yehuda's uncertainty about רכוב ומנהיג
steps.stepSummary: Rav Yosef reports that Rav Yehuda heard from Shmuel that in a case of a rider and a leader, one acquires and the other does not, but Rav Yehuda was uncertain which one acquires.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a report of a halachic uncertainty transmitted through a chain of authorities. Rav יוסף relays Rav יהודה's statement about שמע's uncertainty.
steps.deeperAnalysis: שמע heard two opinions regarding רכוב ומנהיג: one acquires, the other does not. The uncertainty lies in identifying which party acquires. This sets up the central question of the sugya.
steps.whatToRemember: The core uncertainty is not whether one acquires, but WHICH one acquires.
steps.confusionAlert: The text says 'שמעית מיניה דמר שמואל תרתי' - this means שמע heard two opinions, not that he said two things.
steps.whyThisMatters: This uncertainty drives the entire sugya's exploration of acquisition mechanics.

phrases.english: There were two riding.
Rav Yosef said:
Rav Yehuda said to me:

phrases.aramaic: שְׁמַעִית מִינֵּיהּ דְּמָר שְׁמוּאֵל תַּרְתֵּי: רָכוּב וּמַנְהִיג,
phrases.english: I heard from him two things of Mar Shmuel: a rider and a leader,
one acquires and one does not acquire.
And I do not know which of them.

steps.triggerLanguage: היו שנים רוכבין. אמר רב יוסף, אמר לי רב יהודה:
